extracted_ttps.name: Written Content
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Russian threat actor spoofs European Security Events, indicating the creation of deceptive written materials (phishing lures) to support targeting.

extracted_ttps.name: Phishing for Information
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Russian threat actor conducts "Targeted Phishing Attacks" by spoofing European Security Events, aiming to elicit sensitive information.

extracted_ttps.name: Written Content
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Russian threat actors are leveraging a variety of methods & themes used to target and socially engineer users, implying the creation or tailoring of written content to support these social engineering efforts.

extracted_ttps.name: Social Engineering
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Russian threat actors are actively using social engineering techniques, employing a variety of methods and themes, to target users in Microsoft and Google environments.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The attacker establishes communications with victims, initially without malicious content, and only sends a phishing link after the targeted user confirms interest. This technique also involves the creation of dedicated, polished, and professional-looking fake websites to facilitate these attacks.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Service
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The attacker uses messaging apps like WhatsApp/Signal to provide 'live support' and guide targeted users, ensuring they correctly interact with the phishing URL, especially in OAuth phishing workflows.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The user received a spear-phishing email leading to an OAuth authentication workflow. This method required the targeted user to send to the attacker a code generated by Microsoft found in the URL bar of the user's browser, indicative of device code phishing to gain access to the Microsoft 365 account.

extracted_ttps.name: Phishing
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The spear-phishing email employed social engineering by coming from an account the user had recently corresponded with and was a continuation of an existing and legitimate thread, a tactic known as thread hijacking.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The threat actor communicated with the targeted user on WhatsApp under two compromised identities related to the Belgrade Security Conference, priming them for an invitation email. They engaged in rapport-building phishing, sending an email invite with a hyperlink to register for resources tied to the conference, which led to a Microsoft-related page, indicating an attempt to elicit sensitive information via a malicious link.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Adversary sent a hyperlink in an email leading to a Microsoft-owned URL abused for an OAuth phishing workflow. After user login, they landed on a blank webpage with an OAuth token in the URL. The attacker then asked the user to provide this URL to 'finalize registration', successfully stealing the OAuth token and using it to access Microsoft 365 files.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Adversary sent a spearphishing email with a malicious link to a Microsoft-owned URL, which was part of an OAuth phishing workflow. The attacker elicited sensitive information (the OAuth token) by asking the user to provide the URL containing the token under the guise of 'finalizing their registration'.

extracted_ttps.name: Link Target
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Adversary utilized a Microsoft-owned URL (https://login.microsoft[.]com/common/oauth2/authorize?...) as a link target in a spearphishing email. This URL was abused as part of an OAuth phishing workflow, redirecting the user to a blank webpage containing the OAuth token after login.

extracted_ttps.name: Device Registration
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: A new device was created in Microsoft Entra ID to maintain access to the account. The device was given the same name as an existing device belonging to the user. For example, if the user's device was named was ' Charles E. Cheese's iPhone ', the newly registered device would be given the same name. In this specific case, it was notable that the 'iPhone' access was coming from a device claiming to be an Android phone, with the user agent Dalvik/2.1.0 (Linux; U; Android 14; 2211133C Build/UKQ1.230705.002) ;Xiaomi .

extracted_ttps.name: Email Accounts
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Volexity observed broader targeting from an attacker-created Gmail account, indicating the creation of an email account for malicious operations.

extracted_ttps.name: Phishing
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: An attacker-created Gmail account was observed sending several spear-phishing messages to a variety of targets across Volexity's customer base.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Service
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: An attacker-created Gmail account (a third-party service) was used to send spear-phishing messages to targets.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing via Service
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: An attacker-created Gmail account (a third-party service) was used to send spear-phishing messages to targets.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The spear-phishing messages linked to a newly created website, bsc2025[.]org, which masqueraded as a registration page for the Belgrade Security Conference. Users were encouraged to register and prompted to provide a 'Corporate Email'.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The attacker used the 'bsc2025[.]org' website as an initial lure for event registration. If a corporate email domain was of interest, the user was redirected to a Microsoft 365 phishing workflow (e.g., 'https://login.microsoftonline[.]com/common/reprocess?...') where they were prompted to sign in, aiming to elicit credentials.

extracted_ttps.name: Web Portal Capture
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Adversaries are using a fake Microsoft 365 login page, hosted at a URL like https://login.microsoftonline[.]com/common/reprocess?ctx=rQQIARAAJU3P[snipped]m5T2D81&sessionId=941739c9[snipped]2e06bf63f947, to phish users. This webpage is designed to look like a legitimate Microsoft 365 login portal. The only indicator of phishing is a Microsoft Authentication Broker dialogue appearing after authentication. This behavior is linked to the DeviceCodePhishing project, suggesting the capture of credentials or authentication tokens via this malicious web portal.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Service
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Volexity detected spear phishing campaigns aimed at its customers. The initial observed emails invited recipients to participate in an upcoming event called the Brussels Indo-Pacific Dialogue (BIPD). The sender email address was invitationbipd@outlook.com, which is a third-party personal webmail service. Targeted users were individuals working on foreign policy, matters related to Europe, or who had previously worked in senior positions in the United States government. The email used social engineering, posing as 'Eva Blanc' from the 'Centre for Security, Diplomacy and Strategy' at 'Vrije Universiteit Brussel'.

extracted_ttps.name: Email Accounts
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The adversary used the email address "invitationbipd@outlook.com" as the sender for the malicious email, indicating the creation and use of an email account for targeting.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The adversary sent a follow-up email containing a malicious URL, instructing the targeted user to click it to be added to the 'BIPD system'. The URL was unique to the targeted user, indicating a targeted phishing attempt via a link.

extracted_ttps.name: Impersonation
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The adversary impersonated "Eva Blanc, Administrative Assistant of Partnerships & Activities" from "Vrije Universiteit Brussel (VUB)" in the email signature, including contact details, to lend credibility to the malicious request.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The attacker requests that in the event of an error, the user should 'copy and send the exact URL' from the browser, which is consistent with OAuth code phishing techniques.

extracted_ttps.name: Domains
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The domain ustrs[.]com was purchased using a domain auction service or similar. The WHOIS data shows an original create date of 2020-02-05, but it was updated on 2025-10-31, a tactic to defeat reputation-based services that use the age of a domain as a risk indicator.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: An email sender contacted Volexity NSM customers, sharing a link to the dedicated website brussels-indo-pacific-forum[.]org for users to register for a conference. This website was impersonating the BIPD.

extracted_ttps.name: Domains
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The adversary acquired the domain brussels-indo-pacific-forum[.]org to host a dedicated website impersonating the BIPD for conference registration.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Target is presented with a Microsoft 365 Device Code phishing workflow masquerading as a legitimate signup process after visiting a website and attempting to register.

extracted_ttps.name: Web Portal Capture
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Target is presented with a Microsoft 365 Device Code phishing workflow masquerading as a legitimate signup process, designed to capture registration details.

extracted_ttps.name: Compromise Accounts
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Subsequent access to compromised accounts.

extracted_ttps.name: Network Devices
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Subsequent access to compromised accounts takes place via nodes belonging to proxy networks.

extracted_ttps.name: Compromise Accounts
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The threat actor UTA0355 gained subsequent access to compromised accounts, which they accessed via nodes belonging to proxy networks. These compromised accounts were then leveraged to send conference invitations and solicit additional contacts to expand their target list.

extracted_ttps.name: Domains
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: UTA0355 acquired and registered the domain bsc2025[.]org, impersonating the Belgrade Security Conference, using an email account from mailum[.]com. Additionally, UTA0355 registered other domains, including world-nuclear-exhibition-paris[.]com, wne-2025[.]com, and confirmyourflight-parisaeroport[.]com, which masqueraded as the World Nuclear Exhibition.

extracted_ttps.name: Social Engineering
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Threat actors are using "clever socialengineering techniques" including "rapport building phishing" and "the use of fake websites impersonating legitimate websites" to breach accounts.

extracted_ttps.name: Phishing
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Threat actors are employing "rapport building phishing" as part of their social engineering techniques to target Microsoft 365 users and organizations.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Threat actors are using "fake websites impersonating legitimate websites" in conjunction with social engineering and phishing, strongly indicating the use of malicious links to direct victims to these sites for credential harvesting or similar purposes to breach accounts.

extracted_ttps.name: Credentials
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The report states that threat actors are "breaching accounts in this manner" (referring to Device Code/OAuth workflows combined with social engineering, phishing, and fake websites), which directly implies the gathering of credentials or authentication tokens to gain unauthorized access.

extracted_ttps.name: Phishing for Information
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The 'registration' workflow for both the BIPD and BSC events is reminiscent of real-world authentication workflows, where users are asked to perform SSO against various services. Referencing upcoming events as a pretext to engage users has also proven effective, leading to users directly forwarding event invitations.

extracted_ttps.name: Email Accounts
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Threat actors are using compromised accounts to send phishing content. The use of compromised accounts for phishing can lend credibility to an attack, resulting in high success rates for click-throughs.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Service
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: Threat actors are contacting targets via messaging apps such as WhatsApp or Signal ahead of or during a spear-phishing campaign. This provides an additional point of contact and legitimacy to their operations, aligning with the use of third-party services for spearphishing to elicit information or build rapport.

extracted_ttps.name: Spearphishing Link
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The threat actor UTA0355 created websites that mimic real-world events and impersonated individuals associated with those events, which is a common tactic for spearphishing with malicious links.

extracted_ttps.name: Messaging Applications
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: UTA0355 contacted users directly on messaging applications as part of their malicious campaigns.

extracted_ttps.name: Phishing
extracted_ttps.context_indicators: The report excerpt explicitly lists 'PHISHING' as an observed activity. It also mentions 'SOCIAL ENGINEERING', which is a common tactic used in phishing campaigns, and 'OAUTH', indicating that OAuth-related credentials or permissions may have been the target of the phishing.
